The provided context does not contain information regarding Ramchandra Ray's escape or the dynamic between Udayaditya and Sitaram during that event.

Pratapaditya's initial failed plan was to have two Pathan men kill Basanta Ray when he took shelter at the inn in Simultali on his way to Jessore. The method that finally succeeded was a signed death order issued by Pratapaditya, which resulted in a soldier named Abdul killing Basanta Ray with a sword.

The provided context only mentions one disguise (a middle-aged woman) and one primary location (the Mahishi's room, located within the inner quarters) where Ramai Bhand faces physical retaliation from Rammohan Mal.
Mangala (Rukmini) prepared a poison, disguised as medicine meant to win the Yubaraj's heart back from Surma, which caused Surma's death.

The tragic consequence of Vibha's decision to refuse Rammohan's first attempt to escort her to Chandradwip is that King Ramchandra Ray, feeling insulted by the refusal, decided to take another wife; consequently, by the time Vibha eventually arrived, the Maharaj was getting married and offered her no forgiveness.

Bhagavat and Sitaram used a ring brought by Rukmini, which had a seal with the Yubaraj's (Udayaditya's) name engraved on it, to stamp a forged petition. This petition was written to look as if the Yubaraj were lodging a complaint of treason against Pratapaditya with the Emperor to get the kingdom for himself. Bhagavat then presented this petition to Pratapaditya, claiming he had intercepted it from a servant of Udayaditya who had fled the country.

Ramchandra Ray's escape leads Pratapaditya to interrogate the guards, who implicate Udayaditya. When Basanta Ray strongly defends Udayaditya, Pratapaditya's anger increases, though he ultimately views Udayaditya as a weak "fool" who is easily manipulated by those he loves or is subservient to. Consequently, Pratapaditya decides that anyone Udayaditya loves or is subservient to must be separated from him, leading to the decree that the daughter-in-law (Surma) be sent back to her father's house.

The song signifies loneliness, emptiness, and a lack of joy in singing because the loved ones Basanta Ray used to sing for and longed to embrace are no longer there. Basanta Ray sings it first while alone, humming to himself as he gazes at the setting sun, and later to Khan Saheb after Khan Saheb observes that he looks downcast and mentions that they no longer hear his singing.
No relevant content found.

Pratapaditya received Ramchandra Ray with indifference and a lack of joy or haste, ignoring Ramchandra's attempts at conversation to focus on a tax collector's complaint and dismissing him by saying, "Go now, my boy, to the inner quarters." In contrast, Ramchandra later allows and takes immense pleasure in the extreme ridicule of Pratapaditya in his own court, where courtiers describe Pratapaditya's lineage as earthworms and leeches and claim Pratapaditya's father had to plead for anointment from Ramchandra's grandfather.

Rukmini, who is living on the outskirts of Jessore under the name Mangala, re-enters Udayaditya's life when the Rajmahishi, hearing that Mangala knows tantra, mantra, and medicine, sends Matangini to secretly fetch medicine to win the Yubaraj's heart back from Surma. Instead of medicine, Mangala prepares a poison that causes Surma's death.

Udayaditya laments that he is a "weak coward" who burdens others and believes that those who were the "hope of the world" were destroyed for his sake. This feeling of unworthiness leads him to request release from the throne when brought before Pratapaditya. His renunciation culminates in a formal oath taken before Ma-Kali, where he swears that as long as he lives, he will not accept any part of the kingdom, sit on the throne, or touch the royal scepter of Jessore, designating Samaraditya as the heir.
No relevant content found.

Pratapaditya blocked the canal with large sal wood logs to trap the boat, but Rammohan and his followers managed to pull the boat up and over the logs. The attempt further failed because the guards lacked a flintlock, gunpowder, and bullets for their gun, and the man sent to fetch a pursuit boat delayed himself by stopping to smoke a pipe and press someone for the payment of a debt, meaning the pursuit boat arrived too late to catch them.

When Basanta Ray is distressed because no one speaks, he initially plays his sitar "furiously with a great clatter." However, as he sees Vibha's tears, his playing is "greatly hindered," his eyes blur with tears, and his throat becomes choked, forcing him to stop and put the sitar down. Ultimately, his despair is reflected in his decision to leave the sitar behind, stating, "I will not play it anymore."

Earlier, Udayaditya was busy with small tasks, made inquiries and gave advice to poor subjects who brought him gifts, and would have stopped officials from beating subjects without considering the consequences to protect them. After Surma's death, his former courage was gone and he could no longer fight against oppression with all his might; when he saw a royal official beating a subject, he could not bear to see the suffering and quickly ran and entered his house instead of intervening.

The provided text does not describe Vibha's attire when she prepares to receive Ramchandra Ray in Jessore; however, when she seeks him out in Chandradwip, she refuses a palanquin, dresses as a common woman, enters the palace like a maidservant, and is mistaken by the king for a beggar woman seeking alms.
No relevant content found.

The dynamic devolves when Sitaram, who had previously cursed and threatened Rukmini, decides to steal her money under the assumption that she is dead. Upon discovering she is alive, Sitaram attempts to feign affection and make up with her, but Rukmini, enraged and claiming to have returned from the "door of Yama," threatens to put Sitaram in an oven and smear her body with his ashes. The encounter becomes life-threatening when Rukmini searches for a curved blade and attacks the spot where Sitaram stood, forcing him to flee.

Sitaram's strategy begins as a distraction when he and his associates set five or six rooms on fire simultaneously in the evening. His men prolong the chaos by sabotaging firefighting efforts—pouring water where there is no fire, breaking water pots, and creating confusion—which allows Sitaram to lead Udayaditya out of the prison. The strategy evolves into a cover-up after Sitaram's men set fire to Udayaditya's empty prison cell. Sitaram throws bones, a skull, and Udayaditya's sword into the blaze and announces that the roof has collapsed and Udayaditya is unresponsive. This creates the illusion that the Yubaraj died in the fire, a conclusion supported by witnesses who claimed to have seen him through the window or heard his screams, and the discovery of the burnt sword.

Ramchandra Ray was not grateful, feeling that Udayaditya's actions were bound to happen and that it would have been wrong if they had not happened. Additionally, he believed that Udayaditya had saved him for the sake of his own sister, rather than saving the king's life being the main objective.

The mechanism was a forged petition, stamped with the Yubaraj's seal, which made it appear as if the Yubaraj were lodging a complaint of treason against Pratapaditya with the Emperor in order to seize the kingdom for himself; Bhagavat then delivered this petition to Pratapaditya, claiming he had intercepted it from a servant of Udayaditya.

Surma's presence is marked by her stroking Vibha's head and forehead. Her subsequent tragic absence is marked by Vibha's hair remaining unbraided, as Vibha sits quietly waiting for Surma to come and braid it for her, which Surma no longer does.
